coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen/sulfur-doped dual-emission carbon dots with tunable fluorescence for ratiometric sensing of ferric ions and cell membrane imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Caizhen
Xie, Xiaobao
Shi, Qingshan
Feng, Jin
Zhang, Dandan
Huang, Xiaomo
coredata.dc:description: Intrinsic dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) with long wavelength and self-calibration attributes are critical in fluorescence sensing and imaging, but their synthesis remains a great challenge. Here, nitrogen/sulfur-doped dual-emission CDs (NS-CDs) of different sizes were controllably synthesized by solvothermally treatment of sodium alginate (SA) and glutathione (GSH) in formamide. Interestingly, these as-prepared NS-CDs exhibit enchanting dual-emission behavior at 480 and 650 nm, tunable fluorescence, superior photostability, and high nitrogen level up to 19.57%. The decreasing mass ratio of SA/GSH for the synthesis can lead to larger size and lower graphitic nitrogen for NS-CDs, ultimately giving the enhanced photoluminescence (PL) and tunable fluorescence changing from cyan to white-blue. The selected dual-emission NS-CDs showed superior selectivity and sensitivity for ratiometric Fe3+ sensing with a detection limit as low as 0.56 μM because of the aggregation of NS-CDs induced by Fe3+, and achieved the Fe3+ detection in environment water. Moreover, the fluorescence-tunable NS-CDs possessing plenteous hydrophilic groups exhibit excellent biocompatibility and unprecedented localized capability for specific cell membrane imaging. This study designs a facile and economical strategy to prepare dual-emission CDs with long wavlength, tunable fluorescence and high nitrogen level, which present bright prospects in environmental monitoring and cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission CDs
Long wavelength
Tunable fluorescence
Nitrogen/sulfur doped
Ratiometric Fe3+ sensing
Cell membrane imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ in environmental water
Determination of Fe3+ in environmental water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the GDAS ’ Project of Science and Technology Development ( 2019GDASYL-0103013 , 2017GDASCX-0102 , 2018GDASCX-0102 ) and the Third Batch of Xijiang Innovation and Entrepreurship Team Projects in Zhaoqing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological and structural characterization of the NS-CDs. TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of (A) NS-CD1, (B) NS-CD2, and (C) NS-CD3. (D-F) The histograms of the corresponding lateral size distributions from TEM images.
(A) XRD patterns, (B) FTIR specra and (C) the full range XPS spectra of the spectra of the NS-CDs.

(A) C1s high-resolution spectra of (a) NS-CD1, (b) NS-CD2, and (c) NS-CD3. (B) N1s high-resolution spectra of (a) NS-CD1, (b) NS-CD2, and (c) NS-CD3. (C) O1s high-resolution spectra of (a) NS-CD1, (b) NS-CD2, and (c) NS-CD3. (D) S2p high-resolution spectra of (a) NS-CD1, (b) NS-CD2 and (c) NS-CD3.

(A-B) UV–vis absorbance spectra, FL emission spectra and photographs (insets) of NS-CD1 (100 μg·mL−1), NS-CD2 (100 μg·mL−1), and NS-CD3 (100 μg·mL−1) under 365 nm UV light irradiation. (C) FL decay spectra of the three NS-CDs at 480 and 650 nm emission and the CIE chromaticity diagram.

Fluorescence intensity variations of NS-CD3 at different pH environment. (B) Fluorescence spectra of NS-CD3 with adding different concentrations of sodium chloride. (C) Photostability of NS-CDs under irradiation for 1 h.

(A) The fluorescence sensing of NS-CD3 to Fe3+ at (a) various pH values of Tris-HCl solutions, and (b) different temperature (pH = 3.0); (c) time-dependent fluorescent changes of NS-CD3 in the presence of 100 μM Fe3+ (pH = 3.0). (B) The fluorescence changes of NS-CD3 dispersions (pH = 3.0) with various metal ions under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. (C) The ratio of fluorescent intensities (F650/F480) of NS-CD3 at (a) 1 mM of various metal ions, and (b) amino acids or anions. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of NS-CD3 upon the addition of various Fe3+ concentrations under pH 3.0 (a); the dependence of F650/F480 value on the various Fe3+ concentration (the inset shows the linear relationship between the F650/F480 value and the Fe3+ concentration) (b); and the CIE chromaticity diagram of NS-CD3 with different Fe3+ concentration (c).

(A) FL decay spectra of NS-CD3 upon introducing various Fe3+ concentration. Zeta potential values of NS-CD3, NS-CD3/Fe3+, and Fe3+. (C) UV–vis spectra of NS-CD3 in the presence of various Fe3+ concentrations. (D) TEM image and photograph (inset) of the NS-CD3 with the addition of Fe3+. Inset: (a) the NS-CD3 solution; (b) the NS-CD3/Fe3+ system.

(A) Cell viability of HeLa cells after incubation with NS-CDs at different concentrations. (B) Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of HeLa cells incubated (a) without NS-CDs and with (b) NS-CD1, (c) NS-CD2, and (d) NS-CD3. Channel 1, λem = 425–575 nm; Channel 2, λem = 600–700 nm. From left to right: channel 1 (blue channel), channel (red channel), overlay. The scale bars are 10 μm.
Schematic illustration of the solvothermal synthetic route of NS-CDs and their applications for the ratiometric response to Fe3+ and cell membrane imaging.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen/sulfur-doped dual-emission carbon dots with tunable fluorescence for ratiometric sensing of ferric ions and cell membrane imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Microbial Culture Collection and Application, State Key Laboratory of Applied Microbiology Southern China, Institute of Microbiology, Guangdong Academy of Sciences, Guangzhou 510070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Microbial Culture Collection and Application
State Key Laboratory of Applied Microbiology Southern China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Microbial Culture Collection and Application, State Key Laboratory of Applied Microbiology Southern China, Institute of Microbiology, Guangdong Academy of Sciences, Guangzhou 510070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Demay New Materials Technology Co. Ltd., Zhaoqing 526238, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Demay New Materials Technology Co. Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Demay New Materials Technology Co. Ltd., Zhaoqing 526238, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen/sulfur-doped dual-emission carbon dots (NS-CDs) with different sizes, long wavelength, tunable fluorescence, high nitrogen level and high photostability were solvothermally synthesized by treating different mass ratio of non-conjugated precursors of SA and GSH in formamide for ratiometric Fe3+ sensing and targeted cell membrane imaging.

Intrinsic dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) with long wavelength and self-calibration attributes are critical in fluorescence sensing and imaging, but their synthesis remains a great challenge. Here, nitrogen/sulfur-doped dual-emission CDs (NS-CDs) of different sizes were controllably synthesized by solvothermally treatment of sodium alginate (SA) and glutathione (GSH) in formamide. Interestingly, these as-prepared NS-CDs exhibit enchanting dual-emission behavior at 480 and 650 nm, tunable fluorescence, superior photostability, and high nitrogen level up to 19.57%. The decreasing mass ratio of SA/GSH for the synthesis can lead to larger size and lower graphitic nitrogen for NS-CDs, ultimately giving the enhanced photoluminescence (PL) and tunable fluorescence changing from cyan to white-blue. The selected dual-emission NS-CDs showed superior selectivity and sensitivity for ratiometric Fe3+ sensing with a detection limit as low as 0.56 μM because of the aggregation of NS-CDs induced by Fe3+, and achieved the Fe3+ detection in environment water. Moreover, the fluorescence-tunable NS-CDs possessing plenteous hydrophilic groups exhibit excellent biocompatibility and unprecedented localized capability for specific cell membrane imaging. This study designs a facile and economical strategy to prepare dual-emission CDs with long wavlength, tunable fluorescence and high nitrogen level, which present bright prospects in environmental monitoring and cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as novel zero-dimension fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with small size (<10 nm), have attracted considerable scientific interest in various areas due to their combination of the electronic and optical properties of semiconductor quantum dots while avoiding the toxic heavy metals [1,2]. Typically, the CDs consist of a graphitized sp2 carbon core and a shell rich in functional groups (COOH, NH2, and OH, etc), which endow CDs with tunable photoluminescence (PL), high water dispersibility, superior photostability, and low toxicity [3]. These remarkable characteristics make them promising in the fields of sensing, bio-imaging, catalysis, and optoelectronics. However, the synthesis of dual-emission ratiometric CDs with simultaneous long wavlength and tunable fluorescence is still a formidable difficulty. Recently, several reports have demonstrated the key factors of precursors with conjugated structure, reaction solvent and reaction temperature for synthesizing the red and dual-emission CDs. For example, Ru et al. [4] designed blue, yellow, and red emission chiral carbonized polymer dots by solvothermal treatment of L-/D-tryptophan and o-phenylenediamine precursors. Gao et al. [5] developed a solvothermal method to prepare green, yellow and red CDs by S doping and water engineering. Li et al. [6] reported dual-emission CDs by using alizarin carmine as precursor. Despite such inspiring advance, the researches on synthesis of dual-emission CDs with simultaneous long wavelength and tunable fluorescence from non-conjugated precursors are rare, which severly hinders the comprehensive understanding of their PL mechanism and the potential applications.

Since the first discovery in 2004, numerous precursors including chemical compounds and biomass have been used to synthesize CDs for various applications. Sodium alginate (SA), as a linearly natural anionic polysaccharide found in algae and some certain bacteria [7], possesses non-conjugated hexatomic ring structures and abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which offers possibility for constructing dual-emission CDs with unique surface states and optical characteristics. Besides, heteroatom doping (such as nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, and boron) is deemed to be an efficient strategy in regulating the electronic, optical and chemical properties of CDs, and enriching the active sites on their surface [8]. Nitrogen with an atomic size similar to carbon is the most popular dopant to improve the functionalities of CDs for fulfilling various applications [9,10], which can be obtained from the precursors and solvents (like formamide). Meanwhile, the rich COOH groups of SA are liable to react with NH2 groups by amidation, providing high nitrogen level for CDs. Therefore, it is of great opportunity and challeng to prepare dual-emission CDs with long wavelength and tunable fluorescence from non-conjugated SA and nitrogen-contained precursor/solvent. Moreover, the synthesis of dual-emission CDs from SA has not been reported so far.

The development of ratiometric fluorescence analysis in fluorescence sensing and imaging raises the requirement of fluorescence probes from researchers, such as the photostability, biocompability, toxicity and solubility. The emerging ratiometric CDs are becoming desired substitutes of organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in sensing and imaging, which can avoid the interferences from the fluctuation of the light source, probe concentrations, matrix, and sensibility of the instrument by simultaneously measuring the fluorescence intensity ratio of two distinguishably emission peaks at one excitation wavelength (such as dual-emission CDs), thereby enhancing the detection reliability and accuracy [11,12]. Motivated by the superior properties, several reported ratiometric CDs have demonstrated their sensing and imaging capabilities [6,13,14]. However, these ratiometric CDs suffered from cross-sensitivity to multi-targets, high cost, and the requirement of precursors with aromatic structures. Additionally, long-wavelength emission CDs can largely eleminate the auto-fluorescence interfence from biological systems and increase the signal-to-ratio of fluorescence sensing and imaging [15]. Thus, it is highly desirable to design truly facile, economical and controllable strategy to synthesize long-wavlength dual-emission CDs from non-conjugated precursors for specific ratiometric sensing and imaging.

Here, nitrogen/sulfur-doped dual-emission CDs (NS-CDs) with long wavelength and tunable fluorescence were controllably synthesized by treating non-conjugated SA and GSH in formamide at one temperature (Scheme 1 ). Through modulating the mass ratio of SA/GSH, the as-prepared NS-CDs of different sizes exhibit fantastic dual-emission peculiarity at 480 and 650 nm, high nitrogen content of 19.57%, tunable fluorescence varying from cyan to white-blue, and high photostability. Detailed characterizations suggest that the sp2/sp3-hybridized core states and diverse surface states containing nitrogen/oxygen/sulfur relevant active groups trigger the dual-emission property of NS-CDs. Further, upon introduction of Fe3+ into NS-CD systems, their PL intensity at 650 nm was quenched while that at 480 nm kept nearly unchanged due to the aggregation of NS-CDs caused by Fe3+, thus realizing the specific ratiometric Fe3+ sensing. The practical Fe3+ detection in environmetal water was also investigated. Unexpectedly, the fluorescence-tunable NS-CDs with plentiful surface hydrophilic groups were able to localize in the cell membrane, and NS-CDs of higher nitrogen level exhibited stronger discrete fluorescence in cell membrane, which is significantly different from most the reported CDs that can internalizate into the cytoplasm or nucleus. These interesting results reveal the bright future of the obtained NS-CDs in ratiometric Fe3+ detection and cell membrane imaging.

Sodium alginate (SA), reduced glutathione (GSH), formamide used in this work were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was produced using a Milli-Q RiOs Essential ultrapure water system (Merck Milipore, France). Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8), Iron (III) nitrate nonahydrate, Tris, and the amino acides were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). Dialysis membranes (regenerated cellulose, molecular weight cut off (MWCO: 1000)) were purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology (Shang, China). Tris-HCl buffers (20 mM, pH = 3.0) were utilized in all experiment. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Typically, NS-CD1 were synthesized through a one-step microwave-assisted solvothermal process. Sodium alginate (0.51 g) and glutathione (1.06 g) were added into 30 mL formamide, and then stirred with a magnetic stirrer to obtained a homogeneous solution. The mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-line autoclave microwave synthesizer and heated at 160 °C for 2 h, which was stopped by programmed cooling and a dark green solution was obtained. The solution was filtered by 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane, followed by dialysis (MWCO: 1000 Da) against ultrapure water for 72 h to remove excess material, and then freeze-dried to obtain NS-CD1 stored in a dryer. For NS-CD2, and NS-CD3, the synthesis procedures were same as NS-CD1, with a mass ratio of 0.25 and 0.125 of SA/GSH, respectively.

The morphologies, structures and chemical compositions of the NS-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, X-ray Diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8) pattern using Cu kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) at a scan rate of 5°/min, fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra performing on a VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) conducting on a Thermo Escalab 250XI spectrometer (Thermo Fish Scientific, America). The optical properties of NS-CDs and their sensing to Fe3+ in aqueous solutions were analyzed by Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance spectra measured on PerkinElmer Lambda 45 spectrometer, fluorescence emission spectra recorded on a PerkinElmer (FLS 1000), fluorescence lifetime spectra obtained on an Edinburgh Spectrofluorometer FS5. Cell imaging experiments were carried out by a confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM880, ZEISS).

The fluorescence detection experiments were carried out in Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM, pH 3.0) with a NS-CDs concentration of 100 μg·mL−1 to evaluate the fluorescence responsive capacity of NS-CDs to Fe3+. For the selectivity detection, various metal ions salts (including NH4Cl, NaCl, MgCl2, AlCl3, KCl, CaCl2, CrCl3·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Fe(NO)3·9H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CuNO3·xH2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AgNO3, CdCl2·5/2H2O, and Pb(NO3)2), anions (including Na2S·9H2O, Na2SO3, Na2CO3, Na2S2O3, NaClO, NaH2PO4, CH3COONa, KBr, and KI), amino acids (including Gly, Isoleu, Val, Lys, Met, His, Trp, Ala, Thr, Pro, Leu, Ser, Arg, Cys, Asp, Tyr, Glu, and Phe), and some other potential interferents (including H2O2, Glucose, GSH, and EDTA) with a final concentration of 1 mM were separately added into NS-CDs solutions, and Fe3+ (1 mM) were also added into the NS-CDs solutions in the presence of these potential interferents. After incubation for 60 min, the fluorescence intensities were recorded at the emission wavelength of 480 and 650 nm under 420 nm excitation, in which the fluorescence intensities at 480 and 650 nm of NS-CDs solution were defined as F480, and F650, respectively. For the sensitivity detection, various Fe3+ concentration of (0–200 μM) were added into NS-CD systems, followed by incubation for 2 min, the fluorescence emission spectra of all these solutions were recorded. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on the following Eq [16].
(1)
LOD =
3 σ
K
Where K is the slope for the concentration range of Fe3+ of the linearity, σ is the standard deviation of the blank (n = 10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ in environmental water
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water from laboratory and river water from the Pearl River were chosen for the actual water samples. All the water was filtered through 0.22 μm microfitration membrane for further analysis. Various Fe3+ concentrations were spiked to the tap water and Peral River water, and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F650/F480) were recorded under 420 nm excitation. The Fe3+ concentrations in water samples were calculated by the standard equation.

HeLa cells were purchased from ICell Bioscience Inc. HeLa cells were cultured in Minimum essential medium (MEM, Corning), supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS, Biological Industires) and maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2. The effects of the three NS-CDs on cell viability were assessed via Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plate at a density of 5000 cells well−1 and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 in MEM medium. Then the medium was removed and replaced with fresh medium containing NS-CDs in a concentration range of 0–1000 μg·mL−1, and incubated for another 24 h. After removing the NS-CDs solutions, 100 μL medium containing 10 μL CCK-8 solution was added into each well. After the incubation of 1 h, a microplate reader was carried out to recorded the absrobance of each well at 450 nm for further analysis. Cell viability was calculated based on the following Eq. (2).
(2)
Cell
Viability % =
OD
NS-CDs
-
OD medium
OD control
-
OD medium

Cell imaging experiments were carried out to evalluate to the bio-imaging capability of NS-CDs. First, HeLa cells (4 × 104 cells mL−1 in NEM medium) were seeded to the confocal petri dish and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. The fresh medium containing 1 mg mL−1 NS-CDs was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter and then added to the above dish with a diluted concentration of 500 μg mL−1, which was further incubated for 12 h. After removing the medium, the cells were washed three times with PBS (pH≈7.4, 10 mM) to remove extracellular NS-CDs, and then equiped with NEM medium without FBS for fluorescence observation via using a Zeiss LSM 880 laser scanning confocal microscope (λex = 405 nm, λem = 425–550, 600–750 nm).

The non-conjugated precursors with rich nitrogen/oxygen/sulfur related functional groups can provide more reaction sites for the dehydration, polymerization and carbonization to form the sp2/sp3-hybridized domains and various surface states in CDs, synergistically building the multi-emission centers of CDs. Herein, in order to investigate the effect of non-conjugated precursors SA and GSH on the structures and optical properties of dual-emission CDs, three nitrogen/sulfur-doped dual-emission CDs (NS-CDs) were solvothermally synthesized from different mass ratios of SA/GSH (0.5, 0.25, and 0.125) in formamide at 160 °C, which were used as the representatives (NS-CD1, NS-CD2, and NS-CD3) for further characterization and applications. First, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to study their morphological features. As shown in Fig. 1 A-B and D-E, the TEM images and their corresponding diameter distribution histograms display uniform particle size distributions of around 3.05 ± 0.59, and 3.60 ± 0.73 nm for NS-CD1 and NS-CD2, respectively, and their high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1A-B, insets) show clear lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, belonging to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene. The images for NS-CD3 (Fig. 1C and F) show a particle size distribution of approximately 4.13 ± 0.79 nm with a lattice fringe of 0.22 nm, and their dispersion mophology is not completely monodisperse compared with that of NS-CD1 and NS-CD2, probably due to the stronger attractive interaction in NS-CD3. Moreover, HRTEM images show regular augment of carbon core from NS-CD1 to NS-CD3 because of the decrease in SA/GSH mass ratio, suggesting the importance of GSH in enlarging the sp2/sp3-hybridized domains in NS-CDs in the carbonization process. These results demonstrate that the non-conjugated SA and GSH were successfully carbonized into sp2-conjugated CDs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and full X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were carried out to analyze the structure of NS-CDs. As shown in XRD patterns (Fig. 2 A), a broad peak at around 26.5° (002) was found for the three NS-CDs, belonging to an amorphous carbon phase [17]. Besides, two new peaks at 13.54° (001) and 21.78° (002) appear for NS-CD3, where the peak of 21.78° is probably due to the increasing plane distortion from surface functional groups or sp3-C in graphitic structures [18], indicating their different graphitization degree and surface states from that of NS-CD1 and NS-CD2. FTIR results (Fig. 2B) show that hydrophilic functional groups containing OH (3420 cm−1), NH (3195 cm−1), CO (1678 cm−1), and CN (1602 cm−1) were detected for the three NS-CDs, accounting for their good solubility in aqueous environment. The absorption bands at 1426 cm−1, 1381 cm−1 and 1102 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibrations of CC, CN, and CS. Furthermore, the stretching vibration intensities of OH/NH, and CO for NS-CD3 are much lower than that of NS-CD1 and NS-CD2, which is likely due to the lower SA content for the synthesis of NS-CD3. The full XPS spectra of the NS-CDs in Fig. 2C show four typical peaks of C1s (at 285 eV), N1s (400 eV), O1s (531 eV), and S2p (163.48 eV), demonstrating the successful introduction of nitrogen and sulfur elements in NS-CDs. These elemental contents are shown in Table S1. Obviously, the NS-CD1 and NS-CD2 show subtle difference in the C and N contents and the N content is up to 19.57%, indicating the participation of formamide in the formation of NS-CDs. A drop of N content was detected in NS-CD3, suggesting that lower SA content can decrease the amidation in the polycondensation process due to the reduction of COOH groups. The increment of C content in NS-CD3 also proves the growth of graphitization, which agrees well with the TEM results.

The internal structures and possible surface states were further analyzed by high-resolution XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 , the three NS-CDs show similar functional groups. The C1s spectra (Fig. 3A) are composed of three energy bands of CC/CC (284.53 eV), CN/CO/CS (285.78 eV), and CO/OCO (287.73 eV). Irregular changes are found for the CC/CC and CN/CO/CS contents for NS-CD1 to NS-CD3, where the NS-CD2 have the highest CC/CC content and the lowest CN/CO/CS content, while both the CC/CC and CN/CO/CS contents for NS-CD3 increase in comparison with NS-CD1 (Table S2), which indicates the raise of sp2/sp3-hybridized domains and surface defects in NS-CD3 after the carbonization, matching with the TEM and XRD results. Meanwhile, NS-CD1 and NS-CD2 show similar content of CO/OCO (≈27%), whereas that for NS-CD3 decreases to 17.75%, in good agreement with the FTIR data. The N1s spectra (Fig. 3B) could be deconvoled into two pronounced peaks, attributed to graphitic N (399.61 eV) and pyridinic N (398.18 eV), respectively. The graphitic N dominates in NS-CD1 (83.99%) and NS-CD2 (80.66%), but decreases visibly in NS-CD3 (65.32%, Table S3) which were synthesized at the minimum mass ratio of SA/GSH, indicating that fairly low content of SA can restrain the conversion of amino N of formamide into graphitic N in carbon skeleton of NS-CDs in the solvothermal carbonization process. In general, the sp2-conjugated domain and graphitic N are prevalent in the long-wavelength emission CDs [19]. The O1s spectra (Fig. 3C) show two peaks at 532.58 eV (CO) and 531.03 eV (CO). Furthermore, the S2p spectra (Fig. 3D) exhibit a peak of CSC (162.98 eV) for the three NS-CDs, and additional peak of CSO3 sulfonic group at 167.93 eV just appears for NS-CD2 and NS-CD3, demonstrating the improvement of surface oxidation from NS-CD1 to NS-CD3 (Table S5). The above results indicate that the three NS-CDs are composed of the sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon cores and the diverse surface states containing nitrogen/oxygen/sulfur related functional groups.

The optical properties of the three NS-CDs were evaluated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra, and compared in Fig. 4 . The concentration of the NS-CDs was carried out at 100 μg·mL−1. As depicted in Fig. 4A, the UV–vis spectra of NS-CD1 and NS-CD2 in UV region show a broad band centered at around 270 and 268 nm, respectively, while that of NS-CD3 exhibit two well-resolved band at 233 and 261 nm, ascribed to the π-π* transitions of the CC/CN units in sp2-conjugated domains. In the visible region, progressively from NS-CD1 to NS-CD3, the absorption peak at 399 nm slightly shifts to 395 nm and the absorption intensity increases evidently, demonstrating the different surface state transitions on the surfaces of the three NS-CDs [2]. The other three absorption peaks near 423 nm, 643 nm and 690 nm for the three samples are attributed to the n-π* transition of the surface states possessing CO, CN and CS bonds [20,21], associated with the electronic band gap narrowing and long-wavelength emission [5]. These similar absorption bands at 260–700 nm indicate that the three NS-CDs with diverse surface states underwent similar reaction processes [22]. Interestingly, the three NS-CDs of different sizes and graphitization extents show dual-emission characteristic at 480 and 650 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 420 nm, distinct from the multi-color emission CDs possessing different excitation/emission wavelength [18,23]. This unique dual-emission attribute for the three samples might owe to the large sp2/sp3-hybridized domains and the nitrogen/sulfur doping surface states [6,24]. Their fluorescence emission band at short-wavelength region (Em1) exhibit excitation-dependent behavior by moving from 450 nm to 505 nm under the excitation wavelength varying from 360 nm to 450 nm; whereas, the emission centered at 650 nm (Em2) show excitation-independent behavior (Fig. 4B). In general, nitrogen/sulfur doping can alter the electronic structure of both the carbon core and surface states of NS-CDs by tuning the generation of n-type or p-type carriers, resulting in the change of their optical properties [6,8,25]. The various doping forms including graphitic N, pyridinic N, amine, CO, CO, CSC and SO3 can produce rich defect sites in NS-CDs and then might prevent the electron from delocalizing around the whole NS-CDs, thus leading to the multi-emission traps [26]. Besides, the uniform size distributions of the three NS-CDs (Fig. 1) can induce the excitation-independent emission behavior [27]. As a consequence, the uniform size distributions and the abundant emission traps of diverse surface states that contain nitrogen/sulfur-related groups synergistically contribute to the excitation-independent and excitation-dependent features of NS-CDs [17,27,28]. Specially, the three aqueous NS-CDs dispersions show clear and light brown/green color in daylight, and exhibit cyan, baby-blue and white-blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (Fig. 4B, insets), respectively, indicating the fluorescence-tunable property of the NS-CDs. The CIE diagram (Fig. 4C, right) also demonstrate their tunable fluorescence from orange to red under 420 nm excitation, on account of the increasing PL intensity at 650 nm.

The time-resolved PL spectroscopy was further employed in determining their optical behavior (Fig. 4C). The decay curves of 480 nm emission can be fitted into three exponentials and the average fluorescence lifetimes for NS-CD1, NS-CD2 and NS-CD3 were calculated to be 4.50, 4.58, and 4.65 ns, respectively (Table S6-7). For 650 nm emission, a two-component exponential is obtained for the decay curves that contain a fast component (τ1) belonging to the recombination pathways of the core states and a slow component (τ2) ascribed to the surface functional groups [4]. The results for NS-CD1 to NS-CD3 display that their corresponding average lifetimes are about 3.43, 3.10, and 3.01 ns (Table S6-7), where the τ1 proportion grows from 23.29% to 29.75%, suggesting that the core states of the three NS-CDs have an important effect in increasing the PL intensity at 650 nm. The different fitted exponentials also indicate the different emission behaviors between 480 and 650 nm. Additionally, the absolute fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) were calculated to be 4.7%, 5.2%, and 6.5% for NS-CD1, NS-CD2 and NS-CD3, respectively, using an integrating sphere under 420 nm excitation. XPS results have confirm the enhancement of pyridinic N and SO from NS-CD1 to NS-CD3, which might pose increasing density of emission trap sites due to the electron confined effect [22,26]. In addition, the reduction of SA/GSH mass ratio for the synthesis of NS-CD3 causes an increase in the sp2-conjugated domains of core states demonstrated by TEM image (Fig. 1C). Hence, the higher level of surface defects in NS-CD3 of larger sp2-conjugated domains generates a higher QY value.

These findings indicate that the dual-emission NS-CDs of abundant nitrogen/sulfur-containing functional groups and high nitrogen level were formed by going sequently through the similar reactions of dehydration, polymerization and carbonization of the SA, GSH and formamide precursors. Importantly, the three as-prepared NS-CDs exhibit the same dual-emission behaviors at 480 and 650 nm despite of their different sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon cores, graphitic N, and surface oxidation extents. However, their fluorescence are different. In general, enlarging sp2-conjugated domains, introducing nitrogen/sulfur element or adjusting the surface states are able to trigger the excitation/emission redshift of CDs [8,10,29]. In this study, with the decrease of SA/GSH mass ratio for the synthesis of NS-CD1 to NS-CD3, the sp2/sp3-hybridized domains as well as particle size increase while no excitaton/emission redshift are detectable. Nevertheless, the PL intensity at 650 nm progressively from NS-CD1 to NS-CD3 enhances, accompanying by the increasing QYs and tunable fluorescence changing from cyan to white blue. Although the graphitic N are dominant in these three NS-CDs (65–84%, Table S3), a much higher content of electron-accepting pyridinic N is obtained in NS-CD3 compared with that in NS-CD1 and NS-CD2, which might produce more surface defects in NS-CD3 for enhancing the PL intensity [5]. Moreover, the level of SO3 groups for NS-CD1 to NS-CD3 rises from 0% to 11.8% as the surface oxidation degree increases, endowing NS-CD3 with more diverse surface states and in turn improving the emission trap density at 650 nm and the QY. Therefore, the dual-emission characteristic with tunable fluorescence for the NS-CDs is probably due to the synergistic effects of the large sp2/sp3-hybridized core states including graphitic N and C as well as the diverse surface states that contain substantial nitrogen/oxygen/sulfur relevant active groups, which can be achieved through tuning the mass ratio of SA/GSH in formamide.

For further application and development, high stability of NS-CDs under various environment is a prerequisite. NS-CD3 were taken as an example. The pH effect on the PL intensity of NS-CD3 was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the F650/F480 slightly increases in the pH range of 2.0–4.0, keeps a plateau at pH 4.0–7.0, and then increases steeply at pH 8.0–10.0, demonstrating the high acid resistance of NS-CD3. The obvious increase of F650/F480 at alkali environment could be attributed to the deprotonation of the COOH and SO3 groups of NS-CD3. Besides, Fig. 5B depicts that negligible variation was detected for the fluorescence intensities of NS-CD3 at 480 and 650 nm upon the Na+ concentration increasing from 0.1 M to 1 M, suggesting that NS-CD3 have good salt-tolerance. In addition, the NS-CD3 also exhibit high anti-photobleaching under the continuous irradiation time (Fig. 5C). These findings prove that NS-CD3 are highly stable in aqueous solution because of their large sp2-conjugated domain and rich COOH, OH, NH2, and SO3 groups, which is favorable for metal ion sensing and bio-imaging applications.

Ferric ions (Fe3+), an essential trace element for human body, play a critical role in biological systems [30–32]. Also, Fe3+ are one of the major inorganic pollutants in ecological system resulting from the waste discharging from industrial activities, which is a severe threat to human health due to their recyclability in biological chains and non-biodegradability [33,34]. Therefore, it is imperative to monitor the Fe3+ level in environmental systems with sensitive fluorescence probes. Given the dual-emission NS-CDs unique optical properties and abundant functional groups, NS-CD3 of higher QY were chosen as representative to evaluate their feasibility of ratiometric Fe3+ sensing. Prior to studying the ratiometric sensing capacity of NS-CD3 to Fe3+, the sensing environment and response time need to be assessed. As shown in Fig. 6 A-a, NS-CD3 exhibit excellent sensing capacity to Fe3+ in pH 2–3 media, where a higher value of △F [35] (△F is the difference of F650/F480 without and with Fe3+) was achieved at pH 3.0. Therefore, the pH 3.0 was selected to further explore the ratiometric sensing performance of NS-CD3 to Fe3+. After that, the effects of temperature and ion strength on their ratiometric sensing behavior were measured. From Fig. 6A-b, insignificant influence was observed on the sensitivity of NS-CD3 to Fe3+ with the sensing temperature variation. Besides, NS-CD3 still kept their superior sensing capacity at the high Na+ concentration of 1 M (Fig. S1), confirming their remarkable ratiometric sensing performance. In addition, the time-dependence test was also carried out (Fig. 6A-c). Notably, in the presence of Fe3+, there is no change for the fluorescence intensity of NS-CD3 at 480 nm, while that at 650 nm decreases in 1 min, and remains unchanged under extended incubated time, indicating a fast response of NS-CD3 to Fe3+.

Selectivity is always a critical issue for sensing due to the complexity in environmental and biological systems. Thus, the selectivity of NS-CD3 to Fe3+ was investigated in detail (Fig. 6B-C). Fig. 6C illustrates the F650/F480 of NS-CD3 in the presence of various metal ions, anions and molecules (Fe3+, NH4 +, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ag+, ClO-, S2-, S2O3 2-, SO3 2-, CO3 2–, Br-, I-, CH3COO–, H2PO4 -, Gly, Isoleu, Val, Lys, Met, His, Trp, Ala, Thr, Pro, Leu, Ser, Arg, Tyr, Cys, Asp, Glu, Phe, GSH, Glucose, H2O2, and EDTA) at a concentration of 1 mM, respectively. Obviously, only Fe3+ can effectively cause significant decrease of the F650/F480 while the potential interfering ions and molecules shows trivial effect on the F650/F480 (Red bars). Moreover, Fe3+ can still cause nearly the same change on the F650/F480 in coexistence with these potential interferents (dark cyan and green bars), validating the high selectivity of NS-CD3 to Fe3+. Fig. 6B also intuitively reveals the excellent selectivity of NS-CD3 to Fe3+ over other ions. These results demonstrate that the NS-CD3 could act as a superior ratiometric probe for Fe3+ detection in complex media.

Subsequently, the detection sensitivity of NS-CD3 to Fe3+ was studied. As depicted in Fig. 6D-a, the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm decreases regularly with the increasing Fe3+ concentration, and nearly turns off at the concentration of 200 μM, whereas that at 480 nm exhibits subtle change. Fig. 6D-b displays the dependence of F650/F480 on Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μM. Their correlation in the range of 0–100 μM was well fitted into a linear regression equation of F650/F480 = −0.00484C (Fe 3+ ) + 0.70096 (R2 = 0.993), indicating the superior ratiometric capability of NS-CD3. The LOD of Fe3+ was calculated to be around 0.74 μM (S/N = 3). The CIE diagram (Fig. 6D-c) illuminates the fluorescence changes of NS-CD3 dispersions under different Fe3+ concentrations. The above results indicate that NS-CD3 are sensitive, specific and rapid for Fe3+ sensing, making them a promising ratiometric fluorescence probe for practical Fe3+ detection. Besides, NS-CD1 were also used to evaluate their sensing capacity to Fe3+ (Fig. S2-4), and show excellent selectivity to Fe3+ with a LOD of 0.56 μM. Compared with the reported CDs for Fe3+ detection in the literatures (Table S8), both NS-CD1 and NS-CD3 derived from different mass ratio of SA/GSH exhibit superior ratiometric sensing performance with wide linear range and relatively low LOD, indicating the critical roles of COOH, NH2 and OH groups in Fe3+ sensing. Furthermore, the synthetic strategy for dual-emission NS-CDs in this work was facile, economical and green without using expensive materials or further complex surface modification.

Generally, Fe3+ can be prone to attach to hydroxyl groups due to the low solubility product constant (K sp) of Fe(OH)3 (2.8 × 10-39) [33,36], which can act as a bridge for triggering the aggregation of adjacent CDs, thereby changing the fluorescence signal of CDs by electron/charge transfer. FTIR and XPS results have demonstrated the OH, COOH, NH2 and/or SO3 groups enriching the NS-CDs’ surface. To substantiate the sensing mechanism, the time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of NS-CD3 in the presence of various Fe3+ concentrations were first characterized to explore the interaction between NS-CD3 and Fe3+. As displayed in Fig. 7 A and Table S6-7, the decay curves were fitted with two exponential functions. Upon adding 10, 50, 100 μM of Fe3+, the corresponding average decay lifetime (emission at 650 nm) was calculated to be 3.00, 2.84 and 2.78 ns, respectively, which are approximate to that of the blank NS-CD3 dispersion (3.01 ns). This slight change could exclude the strong diffusive collision between NS-CD3 and Fe3+. The zeta potential test was also carried out to deduce the sensing mechanism (Fig. 7B). NS-CD3 exhibit a negative charge of −10.39 ± 0.82 mV in acidic Tris-HCl solution, indicating that NS-CD3 with abundant COOH, OH and NH2 groups could serve as the electron-donor for Fe3+. Then their charge increased to −3.40 ± 1.09 mV after introducing electrophilic Fe3+ (0.46 ± 0.14 mV), which indicates that, in the solution with sufficient acidity (pH 3.0), the electrophilic Fe3+ with half-filled 3d orbitals prefer to interact with COOH, OH and NH2 groups of NS-CD3 via the electron transfer effect [37]. As nitrogen and sulfur elements can facilitate the electron transfer between NS-CD3 and Fe3+ [33], the nonradiative electron-hole recombination is restrained, and in turn results in quenching the fluorescence of NS-CD3 at 650 nm. Moreover, as observed in UV–vis spectra (Fig. 7C), the absorbance intensities of NS-CD3 at 395 and 423 nm decreased gradually with the increase of Fe3+ concentrations, presumably because Fe3+ caused the aggregation of NS-CD3 through the chelation. To verify this inference, the TEM was used to examine the dispersion morphology of NS-CD3/Fe3+ (Fig. 7D). It is clearly observed by naked eye that the NS-CD3 are highly water-soluble, but a great number of precipitates arose after the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 7D-a/b, inset), which matched with the large aggregates of random shape illustrated in the TEM image. These results indicate that the electron-donating groups of COOH, OH and NH2 from NS-CD3 can chelate with the electrophilic Fe3+ by the electron transfer effect, acting as a bridge for inducing the adjacent NS-CDs to assemble into large aggregates of diverse extents, such as the uneven dark aggregates composed of numerous NS-CD3 and Fe3+. This behavior leads to the reduction of the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm and the intensity at 480 nm serving as a reference shows insignificant change, thus achieving the ratiometric fluorescence response to Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ in environmental water
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability of NS-CD3 for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Fe3+ in environmental samples, the Pearl River water and tap water samples were detected with the standard addition method. The original iron content of each control sample was measured by inductively coupled mass spectroscopy (ICMPS) [38]. Then, the recovery experiments were implemented by spiking different Fe3+ concentration into the water samples. As shown in table S9, the recoveries of Fe3+ in the Pearl River water and tap water samples were calculated to be around 96–105%, and 99–104%, respectively. Their relative standard deviation (RSD) for each sample was under 5%. These satisfactory results manifest that NS-CD3 present a bright prospect for monitoring Fe3+ in environmental water due to their excellent ratiometric sensing characteristic for avoiding the analyte-independent interferences.

Before exploring the bio-imaging application, the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared NS-CDs was estimated by the standard CCK-8 assay. Fig. 8 A depicts that over 95% HeLa cells were alive after incubation with the three NS-CDs at various concentrations ranging from 100 to 1000 μg·mL−1 for 24 h, indicative of the insignificant cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of NS-CDs, which is highly beneficial for their bio-imaging application. Then, the confocal laser scanning microscopy was utilized to evaluate the visual imaging of NS-CDs in living cells. After incubation with NS-CD1, NS-CD2, and NS-CD3 at 500 μg·mL−1 for 12 h, respectively, the fluorescence images of HeLa cells were obtained by collecting the dual-channel emission fluorescence at 405 nm laser excitation. As shown in Fig. S5, no apoptosis and damage of HeLa cells were observed upon treating with the NS-CDs, also demonstrating their good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Contradistinction with the HeLa cells without NS-CDs treatment (Fig. 8a), the ones incubated with NS-CD1, NS-CD2, and NS-CD3 (Fig. 8b-d) show bright blue and red fluorescence, confirmation of the dual-emission capacity of NS-CDs in living cells. In general, zero-dimensional CDs could efficiently enter into the cytoplasm or nucleus of living cells by endocytosis or passive diffusion/transport [39]. However, the three NS-CDs discretely distribute in the outer membrane, suggesting their targeted capability for specific cell membrane imaging. FTIR, and XPS results have confirm that the hydrophilic groups are the majority on the surface of NS-CDs that show negative charges in aqueous environment (pH≈7.2, Fig. S6), indicating that the hydrophobic interaction is not the driving force for the NS-CDs enriching in cell membrane. Thus, the discrete fluorescence in the boundary of HeLa cells is probably due to the specific interaction of NS-CDs with the proteins in cell membrane through the electrostatic attraction. Note that NS-CD1 and NS-CD2 have stronger fluorescence in the cell membrane because of their higher NH2/OH contents. The results suggest that the obtained NS-CDs with high photostability, low cytotoxicity, and superior biocompatibility could serve as fluorescence probes for cell membrane imaging.

In summary, the successful preparation of nitrogen/sulfur-doped NS-CDs with dual-emission behavior at 480 and 650 nm, and tunable fluorescence was demonstrated. Basically, the dual-emission NS-CDs of different sizes show high nitrogen content up to 19.57%, enhancing PL intensity at 650 nm with tunable fluorescence changing from cyan to white-blue and high photostability by tuning the non-conjugated precursor mass ratio of SA/GSH in formamide. The results indicate that the dual-emission mechanism of the NS-CDs is probably due to the extensive sp2/sp3-hybridized core states and the diverse surface state containing nitrogen/sulfur relevant functional groups. Inspired by the alluring properties, the obtained NS-CDs with abundant active groups were proved to be brilliant ratiometric probes for Fe3+ sensing due to the aggregation of NS-CDs induced by Fe3+. The detection limit was determined to be as low as 0.56 μM. Furthermore, the selected ratiometric NS-CDs also realized the practical Fe3+ detection in the Pearl River and tap water samples. Surprisingly, the fluorescence-tunable NS-CDs, possessing advantageous biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, display specific cell membrane targeted capacity by discretely distributing in the outer cellular membrane, different from other reported CDs that could be internalized into the cytoplasm and/or the cell nucleus. Therefore, this work develops a simple and feasible approach to synthesize dual-emission CDs with simultaneous long wavelength and tunable fluorescence for Fe3+ sensing and cell membrane imaging.

Caizhen Liang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xiaobao Xie: Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Qingshan Shi: Supervision, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Jin Feng: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Software. Dandan Zhang: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xiaomo Huang: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the GDAS’ Project of Science and Technology Development (2019GDASYL-0103013, 2017GDASCX-0102, 2018GDASCX-0102) and the Third Batch of Xijiang Innovation and Entrepreurship Team Projects in Zhaoqing.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.151447.

Interfaces, 12 (2020) 47245-47255.
